name: Formative Communication-Skills Scoring Supports Hybrid Synthetic Training With Real Evaluation
statement: Rezayi et al. support synthetic learner responses as lower-stakes formative training augmentation when models are evaluated on real annotated learner responses; they explicitly state that synthetic data reduces but does not replace real data collection and that summative contexts require human-rater review for borderline or below-passing responses.
population_or_context: Learner physicians practicing communication skills through simulated patient interactions.
time_horizon: Full text retrieved and extracted on 2026-06-03.
quantitative_note: Table 3 reports average binary F1 of 0.878 for 15 real plus 50 synthetic examples compared with 0.793 for 15 real only; synthetic-only training averaged 0.757 compared with 0.723 baseline.
caveats: The primary use case is formative feedback and communication-skills development.
The study covers 8 clinical scenarios and may not generalize to other domains or high-stakes uses.
The authors identify real annotated responses as necessary for robust evaluation.
evaluation_method: 5-fold cross-validation at the case level and binary F1 on real learner responses.
reported_metrics: Binary F1 by case and average F1 across training scenarios.
limitations: Does not validate synthetic-only scoring for summative decisions.
Summative deployment is framed as a hybrid model with human review of borderline or below-passing responses.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the synthetic training setup, F1 comparisons, real-evaluation limitation, and ethical considerations for formative versus summative contexts.
